author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The 'divine dilemma' — God's goodness cannot let His handiwork perish. NOTE: this file adds the OTHER sections of the treatise; the famous 'great exchange' at §54 ('For He was made man that we might be made God…') already lives in patristics.json as id 'athanasius-inc-54' — do not duplicate it. Verified verbatim against source_url ('God's goodness' confirmed; apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For it were not worthy of God's goodness that the things He had made should waste away, because of the deceit practised on men by the devil.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why repentance alone could not restore humanity — it cannot recall men from their nature nor satisfy God's just claim. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: But repentance would, firstly, fail to guard the just claim of God. For He would still be none the more true, if men did not remain in the grasp of death; nor, secondly, does repentance call men back from what is their nature—it merely stays them from acts of sin.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Word 'takes unto Himself a body, and that of no different sort from ours' — a real, consubstantial humanity, not an appearance. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: He takes unto Himself a body, and that of no different sort from ours.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The Word restoring the image of God in man so that men might know Him again. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: What then was God to do? or what was to be done save the renewing of that which was in God's image, so that by it men might once more be able to know Him?

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The portrait analogy — the effaced likeness renewed only by the one whose likeness it is coming again in person. Robertson's NPNF wording (NOT the copyrighted CSMV/Popular Patristics 'You know how it is when some portrait…'). The full sentence begins 'For as, when the likeness…'; the quote below preserves the load-bearing clause. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: When the likeness painted on a panel has been effaced by stains from without, he whose likeness it is must needs come once more to enable the portrait to be renewed on the same wood.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why the death was public and not a private, natural death — a hidden death would have been mistaken for mere human weakness. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: If, then, He had laid aside His body somewhere in private, and upon a bed, after the manner of men, it would have been thought that He also did this agreeably to the weakness of His nature, and because there was nothing in him more than in other men.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: Why the death had to be on the cross specifically — to bear the curse ('Cursed is he that hangeth on a tree') and, with outstretched hands, to draw Jew and Gentile alike. The ellipsis marks the omitted span between the two sentences within §25. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For if He came Himself to bear the curse laid upon us, how else could He have 'become a curse,' unless He received the death set for a curse? and that is the Cross. For this is exactly what is written: 'Cursed is he that hangeth on a tree.' … Whence it was fitting for the Lord to bear this also and to spread out His hands, that with the one He might draw the ancient people, and with the other those from the Gentiles, and unite both in Himself.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The reality of the resurrection argued from the fact that death is now trampled underfoot by all who belong to Christ. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For if, as our argument shewed, death has been brought to nought, and because of Christ all tread him under foot, much more did He Himself first tread him down with His own body, and bring him to nought.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The living Christ known by His ongoing acts — a dead man works nothing, yet Christ still expels demons and overturns idolatry. Verified verbatim against source_url (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-11).
text: For it is plain that if Christ be dead, He could not be expelling demons and spoiling idols; for a dead man the spirits would not have obeyed.
